questions.promptTex: \text{The full group you want to study is the:}
hints: The whole group = population.
Sample is the subset.
questions.explanation: Population = the whole group. Sample = the subset you observe.
steps: **Population:** entire group of interest.
**Sample:** subset actually observed.

choices: every member has equal chance of being chosen
hints: SRS = equal probability for everyone.
It's the gold standard for unbiased sampling.
questions.explanation: SRS: every member of the population has the same probability of being selected (and every subset of size n is equally likely).
steps: Simple random sample: every person in the population has the same chance of selection.
(Equivalent: every possible sample of size $n$ is equally likely.)
Volunteer/convenience samples are NOT random — they self-select.

questions.promptTex: \text{Asking only people leaving a gym about their exercise habits suffers from:}
hints: You're systematically excluding non-gym-goers.
Selection bias: the sample frame misses part of the population.
questions.explanation: **Selection bias.** Gym attendees are systematically different from non-attendees regarding exercise — your sample is not representative of the general population.
steps: The sampling frame (gym exit) excludes non-exercisers.
Result: sample is biased toward exercisers.
This is **selection bias** — the method itself excludes part of the population.

questions.promptTex: \text{A school has } 60\% \text{ underclassmen and } 40\% \text{ upperclassmen.} \\ \text{To survey, you randomly pick 60 underclassmen and 40 upperclassmen.} \\ \text{This is a:}
hints: You sampled separately from groups (strata) in proportion.
Stratified.
questions.explanation: Sampling separately from defined subgroups (strata) → **stratified sampling**.
steps: You divided the population into two groups (strata): underclassmen and upperclassmen.
Then sampled randomly from each, in proportion to their share.
**Stratified sampling.**

questions.promptTex: \text{A poll asks people their political views over the phone. Many refuse to answer.} \\ \text{The respondents may differ from non-respondents. This is:}
hints: The bias comes from people choosing NOT to respond.
Non-response bias.
questions.explanation: **Non-response bias** — people who refuse to answer may systematically differ from those who do, distorting the result.
steps: Even if the call list is unbiased, the people who choose to answer might differ.
(E.g., busy professionals refuse; retirees answer.)
**Non-response bias.**

questions.promptTex: \text{A survey asks 'Have you ever shoplifted?' Many people lie. This is:}
hints: Sensitive questions → people give inaccurate answers.
Response bias.
questions.explanation: **Response bias** — people give inaccurate answers due to social pressure or sensitive topic. Common with questions about illegal/embarrassing behavior, salaries, or political views.
steps: Respondents answer, but their answers are systematically wrong.
Cause: social desirability — people lie about embarrassing or illegal things.
**Response bias.**

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is the BEST way to fix bias in a study?}
choices: Take a much larger biased sample
Use random sampling from the start
hints: Bigger biased sample is still biased!
Random sampling fixes bias at the source.
questions.explanation: **Random sampling from the start** is the only reliable fix. A larger biased sample is still biased — sample size doesn't cure bias.
steps: Bias is a **systematic** error in how you collected data.
Larger sample reduces *random* error, but cannot remove *systematic* error.
Only **random sampling** ensures the sample resembles the population.

questions.promptTex: \text{The 1936 Literary Digest poll (10M ballots, 2.4M responses) wrongly predicted Landon would beat Roosevelt. Why?}
choices: They sampled from phone books and car registries — biased toward wealthier voters
hints: 10 million is huge — but biased.
In 1936, owning a phone or car was a wealth indicator.
questions.explanation: Selection bias: phone and car owners in 1936 were disproportionately wealthier — and wealthier voters in 1936 favored Landon. Sample was huge but systematically off.
steps: 10 million ballots — sample was MASSIVE.
But: phone/car owners in 1936 = wealthier than average.
Wealthier voters supported Landon; the broader public supported Roosevelt.
**Selection bias** → wrong prediction despite huge sample size.
Lesson: bias matters more than size. Gallup's smaller-but-random poll got it right.

questions.promptTex: \text{The } \textbf{population mean} \text{ is denoted by } \_\_\_, \text{ and the } \textbf{sample mean} \text{ by } \_\_\_.
hints: Population: Greek letters (parameters). Sample: English letters with bars (statistics).
$\mu$ for population, $\bar{x}$ for sample.
questions.explanation: Population mean: $\mu$ (Greek). Sample mean: $\bar{x}$ (x-bar). Standard convention.
steps: Population parameter: usually a Greek letter (μ for mean, σ for sd).
Sample statistic: usually English letter, often with a bar ($\bar{x}$ for mean, $s$ for sd).
We use sample statistics to estimate population parameters.

questions.promptTex: \text{Picking every 10th person from a sorted list is:}
hints: Picking every kth member = systematic.
Watch out: bad if the list has periodic pattern.
questions.explanation: **Systematic sampling.** Simple, but problematic if the list has a hidden cycle that aligns with your interval.
steps: Picking every $k$th element from an ordered list = systematic sampling.
Easy to implement. But beware: if there's a hidden periodic pattern in the list at interval $k$, you'll get a biased sample.

questions.promptTex: \text{An online poll on a news website lets visitors vote. Which best describes the sample?}
hints: Anyone visiting the site and choosing to vote — self-selected.
Convenience/volunteer sample, classic source of bias.
questions.explanation: Visitors self-select to answer. **Convenience / volunteer sample** — heavily biased toward people with strong opinions and time to vote.
steps: The sample is whoever (a) visits the site and (b) chooses to vote.
Both are self-selected — not random.
Convenience/volunteer samples are heavily biased toward people with strong feelings about the topic.
Online polls are notoriously unreliable for this reason.

questions.promptTex: \text{Doubling the sample size in a randomly-collected sample:}
choices: Decreases random error (variability) by a factor of about $\sqrt{2}$
hints: Standard error scales as $1/\sqrt{n}$.
$\sqrt{2 \cdot n}$ vs $\sqrt{n}$ → factor $\sqrt{2}$ improvement.
questions.explanation: Larger sample → less random variability. Standard error scales as $1/\sqrt{n}$, so doubling $n$ improves precision by $\sqrt{2}$. Bias unchanged.
steps: Standard error of the mean: $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.
Double $n$: new SE = $\sigma/\sqrt{2n} = (\sigma/\sqrt{n})/\sqrt{2}$.
So precision improves by $\sqrt{2}$ ≈ 1.41×.
(Note: bias is a property of the sampling method, not the size — doubling doesn't fix it.)
